planting.note: Plant in spring for a tall bark-feature tree.

directions: Grown mainly for spectacular red-brown exfoliating bark, this tall fauriei selection reaches tree size fast. Limb it up as it grows to show off the trunks. Small white flower clusters appear in summer as a secondary feature.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a tall bark specimen tree.

directions: This fauriei selection is prized for rich cinnamon-brown bark on a large upright frame. Remove lower branches over time to reveal the smooth trunks. White flowers open in summer, but the bark carries interest year round.

planting.note: Plant in spring; cold-tolerant white tree form.

directions: A cold-hardy white-flowered hybrid similar to Natchez but tougher in marginal zones. Train it to several trunks to show the exfoliating bark. Clean white flowers bloom all summer with strong red-orange fall foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring where a tall red tree can develop.

directions: Sold as Red Rocket, this vigorous hybrid carries large bright-red flower clusters on a fast, upright tree. Give it full sun and room to grow tall. Big red panicles bloom through summer and rebloom if spent heads are trimmed.

planting.note: Plant in spring for burgundy foliage and pink bloom.

directions: Sold as Pink Velour, this compact hybrid pairs wine-red new foliage with bright pink flowers. The dark leaves hold their color best in full sun. Neon-pink panicles bloom over a long summer against burgundy-tinged leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring for dark leaves and soft pink bloom.

directions: Sold as Rhapsody in Pink, this hybrid has smoky burgundy foliage and light pink flowers. The dark leaves make a striking backdrop for the soft blooms. Pale pink panicles open through summer over near-black new growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring for near-black foliage; a Black Diamond type.

directions: Part of the dark-leaved Black Diamond group, this selection holds nearly black foliage all season. Plant in full sun to keep the leaf color deep. Red flowers pop dramatically against the dark leaves from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring for dark foliage and blush-white bloom.

directions: Another dark-leaved Black Diamond selection, this one pairs near-black foliage with soft blush-white flowers. Full sun keeps the leaves darkest. Pale pink-white panicles bloom all summer against the deep foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring for black leaves and dark red bloom.

directions: A Black Diamond selection with dark foliage and deep red flowers. Plant in full sun and avoid heavy shade, which fades the leaf color. Rich red panicles bloom from early summer to frost above near-black leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring; true dwarf for edging and pots.

directions: This true dwarf forms a low, spreading mound ideal for edging, containers, and small beds. Shear lightly in early spring to keep it dense and tidy. Deep rose-pink flowers bloom through summer with bronze-red fall foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; miniature suited to pots and rock gardens.

directions: One of the smallest crape myrtles, this miniature makes a tight bun perfect for containers and rock gardens. Give it full sun and sharp drainage. Pink-lavender flowers dot the tiny mound through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; compact dwarf for small spaces.

directions: A compact dwarf that fits foundation beds and large containers without pruning to control size. Keep it in full sun for the best flowering. Dark red flowers bloom heavily through the summer on a dense little frame.

planting.note: Plant in spring; dwarf purple for beds and pots.

directions: A dwarf selection grown for bright purple flowers on a compact rounded shrub. It suits containers and low mass plantings. Vivid purple panicles bloom through summer with orange fall foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring for bicolor red-and-white bloom.

directions: Sold as Raspberry Sundae, this upright hybrid bears striking red-and-white bicolor flowers. New foliage flushes burgundy before turning green. Candy-striped panicles bloom through summer on a narrow small tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a tall deep-purple tree.

directions: A tall, vigorous selection prized for some of the darkest purple flowers among crape myrtles. Grow it in full sun as an upright small tree. Deep violet-purple panicles bloom through summer over dark green leaves.

planting.note: Transplant nursery stock in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Plant at the same depth it grew in the container and water deeply while roots establish. This selection resists leaf spot and carries long lavender-blue flower spikes; shear spent blooms to prompt a second flush. Expect showy panicles from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Set out in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Choose a hot, sunny spot with sharp drainage and avoid rich or soggy ground. Prized for unusually deep purple flower spikes, it blooms on new wood, so cut it back firmly at winter's end. Trim faded spikes to extend the summer show.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost date.

directions: Give it full sun and gritty, well-drained soil for the best flower color. Known for dense, dark blue spikes, it flowers reliably on the current year's shoots after a hard late-winter pruning. Deadhead to keep new spikes forming into late summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring once frosts have ended.

directions: Set in full sun with free-draining soil and water only until established, as it tolerates drought well. This form offers soft pink flower spikes instead of the usual blue and blooms across summer. Removing spent panicles encourages repeat flowering.

planting.note: Plant out in spring after frost.

directions: Plant in a sunny, sheltered site with soil that never stays wet. This white-flowered selection lights up the border with pure ivory spikes and pairs well with the blue forms. Prune hard in late winter and deadhead to prolong the display.

planting.note: Set out in spring when soil warms.

directions: Site it in full sun with lean, dry soil to bring out strong flowering. This selection carries clear pink spikes over gray-green aromatic foliage through summer. Cut back stems in late winter since flowers form on new growth.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained ground where its compact, tidy habit suits smaller yards. It bears rich blue-violet spikes over a long midsummer season. Shear lightly after the first flush to invite more blooms.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost danger has passed.

directions: This dwarf grows to roughly a third the size of the species, fitting neatly in a mixed border or large container. Plant in full sun and sharp-draining soil, then prune hard in early spring to shape it. Fragrant lavender-blue spikes appear from early summer onward.

planting.note: Set out after the last spring frost.

directions: Grown as much for foliage as flowers, its leaves show deep purple undersides that flash in the breeze. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and prune in late winter to keep it dense. Purple-blue flower spikes rise above the two-toned leaves in summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring when soil has warmed.

directions: A more compact, well-branched form that stays full without much shaping. Give it full sun and dry, well-drained soil, and cut it back in late winter for the strongest flowering. Dense blue spikes cover the plant through the summer months.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost.

directions: This tidy dwarf holds upright pink flower spikes on a compact, rounded frame ideal for small spaces. Plant in full sun with fast-draining soil and trim in early spring. Reblooming through summer, it draws bees and butterflies to every spike.

planting.note: Set out in spring once frosts end.

directions: Plant in full sun and lean soil where good air movement discourages leaf disease. This selection offers full, medium-blue spikes and an upright, tree-like habit. Prune hard at winter's end and remove spent flowers to keep it blooming.

planting.note: Plant young stock in spring after frost.

directions: This broad-leaved variety is among the hardier and more vigorous forms, often grown as a small multi-stemmed tree. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and limb it up over time for a canopy. Large lavender spikes open across summer and attract pollinators.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after the last frost.

directions: Give this vigorous selection full sun and dry, well-drained soil for heavy flowering. It produces long, deep violet-blue spikes and a strong upright form. Cut back firmly in late winter and shear spent blooms to encourage a second round.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring after frost.

directions: This variety carries finely dissected, fern-like leaflets that give it a lacy texture. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, cutting it back in late winter to keep it shapely. Airy lilac flower spikes open through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant in spring in frost-free or nearly frost-free areas.

directions: This tender shrub suits warm coastal and subtropical gardens and dislikes hard freezes. Plant in full sun with free-draining soil and water through dry spells while young. Blue-purple flower spikes rise above aromatic three-part leaves in summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost in warm regions.

directions: Grown for foliage that is green above and rich purple beneath, giving a two-toned effect in wind. Plant in full sun with warm, well-drained soil and protect from hard frost. Lavender-blue flower spikes contrast against the purple-backed leaves in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in mild coastal climates; note it is invasive in parts of the U.S. Southeast.

directions: This low, sprawling shrub roots along trailing stems and tolerates salt spray and sandy soil. Plant only where permitted, as it spreads aggressively over coastal dunes and is regulated in several states. Small blue-purple flowers appear on rounded silvery leaves in summer, followed by dark fruits.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring or autumn in a sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: Plant this deciduous shrub in well-drained soil against a warm wall so its late-winter blooms are protected. Prune only lightly right after flowering, since flowers form on the previous year's wood. Expect waxy, spicy-scented pale yellow flowers with maroon centers to open on bare branches from midwinter into early spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring or fall where late-winter bloom can be enjoyed up close.

directions: Give this cutting-grown selection a sheltered, sunny position and free-draining soil. It differs from the species in bearing clear yellow flowers without the inner purple staining, and it opens slightly later. Cut a few flowering stems for indoors in late winter to enjoy the strong fragrance.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring or autumn in a warm, sheltered site.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil sheltered from cold winds. This form carries larger, deeper yellow flowers marked with red inside than the ordinary species. Watch for the scented blooms on leafless stems through the coldest months.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring or fall in a sheltered, sunny border.

directions: Choose a warm, protected spot with well-drained ground for this vegetatively propagated selection. Its large flowers are uniformly soft yellow, lacking any purple in the throat. Prune sparingly after bloom to keep an open framework of flowering wood.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring or autumn against a warm wall.

directions: Site this selection in sheltered, sunny ground with sharp drainage. It is valued for deep, richly colored yellow flowers and reliable winter fragrance. Enjoy the blooms on bare wood from midwinter and prune only to shape once they fade.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot with protection from hard frost.

directions: Grow this semi-evergreen shrub in humus-rich, well-drained soil with light shade in hot regions. Its glossy leaves stay late and it bears small, softly fragrant creamy-white flowers in autumn rather than deep winter. Shelter young plants from cold drying winds while they establish.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, sheltered ground with some afternoon shade.

directions: Set this narrow-leaved, semi-evergreen species in fertile, moisture-retentive soil that drains freely. The slender willowy foliage is aromatic when crushed, and small pale flowers appear in late summer and autumn. Keep it out of exposed, frost-prone pockets.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered position with good drainage.

directions: This evergreen species prefers a mild, protected site in humus-rich, well-drained soil. It carries small creamy-white fragrant flowers in the cooler months against leathery green leaves. Mulch the root zone and shelter it from cold winds in borderline climates.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot in mild-winter regions.

directions: Give this evergreen Chinese species moist but well-drained, acid-to-neutral soil and light shade. Small fragrant whitish flowers open in autumn among the glossy foliage. Best in frost-free or nearly frost-free gardens where the leaves are not scorched.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring or fall in dappled shade with acidic soil.

directions: Plant this compact, spreading shrub in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in light shade, sheltered from late frosts. It is the daintiest winterhazel, hanging short two- to three-flowered racemes of pale primrose bells in early spring before the leaves. Avoid limey soils and drying winds, and mulch to keep roots cool.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring or autumn in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Set this wide, spreading shrub in moist but well-drained acidic soil with light shade and shelter from cold spring winds. Its drooping racemes of soft yellow flowers, set off by reddish-purple anthers, open on bare wood in early spring. Prune only to remove crossing branches after flowering to preserve its layered form.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich soil.

directions: This is among the largest and hardiest winterhazels, so give it room in moist, well-drained acidic soil with light shade. Fragrant pale yellow flowers hang in racemes on the bare branches in early to mid spring. Water during dry spells while young and mulch to conserve moisture.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring or fall in sheltered, acidic ground.

directions: Grow this vigorous shrub in moist, free-draining acidic soil in light woodland shade, protected from late frosts. Long drooping racemes of scented soft yellow flowers cover the bare stems in early spring. Give it space to develop its arching, layered branch structure.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring or autumn in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: This botanical variety of Chinese winterhazel wants the same cool, moist, acidic and humus-rich conditions in dappled shade. Its nearly hairless leaves accompany pendulous racemes of fragrant pale yellow flowers in early spring. Shelter it from frost pockets and keep the roots mulched.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring or fall in sheltered, acidic soil.

directions: This cutting-grown selection is prized for its plum-purple young shoots and bronzed emerging leaves. Plant it in moist, well-drained acidic soil in light shade for the best foliage color, and shelter it from late frosts. Pale yellow scented flowers open on bare wood in early spring, followed by the striking purple new growth.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant this refined winterhazel in cool, moist, well-drained acidic soil in light shade. Its primrose-yellow flowers are distinguished by contrasting brick-red anthers on drooping early-spring racemes. Protect the opening blooms from hard late frosts and mulch to retain soil moisture.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring or fall in sheltered, acidic ground.

directions: This Japanese winterhazel resembles the fragrant winterhazel and enjoys the same moist, acidic, well-drained soil in light shade. Pale yellow scented racemes hang from the leafless stems in early spring, with blue-green foliage following. Shelter it from cold winds and late frosts for the best display.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Set this compact, hardy winterhazel in moist, humus-rich acidic soil in dappled shade. Fragrant pale yellow flowers open on bare branches in early spring, and the rounded leaves color softly in autumn. Mulch the roots and shelter the plant from drying winds.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring or fall in sheltered, acidic soil.

directions: Grow this spreading Chinese winterhazel in cool, moist, well-drained acidic soil in light shade. It bears drooping racemes of soft yellow, broad-petaled flowers on bare stems in early spring. Give it plenty of lateral room and protect the blooms from late frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, acidic site in milder regions.

directions: This large winterhazel wants moist, humus-rich, well-drained acidic soil in light shade and a mild, sheltered garden. Long racemes of scented pale yellow flowers open in early spring, and the boldly veined leaves are handsome through summer. Shelter it well, as it is less cold-tolerant than the Japanese species.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: This garden-raised hybrid, propagated from cuttings, is planted in moist, humus-rich acidic soil in light shade. It forms a graceful, spreading shrub hung with fragrant pale yellow racemes on bare wood in early spring. Shelter the flowers from late frost and mulch to keep the roots cool and moist.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, acidic site in mild-winter gardens.

directions: This tender Chinese winterhazel needs a warm, sheltered garden with moist, well-drained acidic soil and light shade. As its name suggests, it carries long, densely flowered racemes of pale yellow blossoms in late winter to early spring. Protect it from frost and cold wind, which can damage both flowers and young foliage.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring or fall in acidic, humus-rich soil.

directions: Grow this vigorous winterhazel, often treated as a form of the Chinese species, in cool, moist, well-drained acidic soil in light shade. Slender drooping racemes of soft yellow, fragrant flowers open on bare stems in early spring. Give it room to arch and shelter the early blooms from frost.

planting.note: Set out container or balled plants in early spring or fall while the ground is workable.

directions: Plant with the root flare level with the surrounding soil in moist, humus-rich ground and spread an organic mulch over the roots. Water through the first two summers and prune only lightly right after flowering to keep the natural vase shape. Ribbon-like yellow flowers open on the bare autumn branches just as the leaves drop, releasing a soft spicy scent.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in a moist spot where its suckering habit has room to spread.

directions: Site this suckering shrub in consistently moist soil and plant at the same depth it grew in the nursery. Remove unwanted root sprouts each year if you want to contain its width, and mulch to keep the roots cool. Small, intensely fragrant orange-to-red flowers cluster along the stems in late winter, often perfuming the whole garden.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or autumn in neutral to acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Choose a sheltered spot with moist but well-drained soil and set the plant so the root collar sits at ground level. Feed with a leaf-mold mulch and prune sparingly after bloom to preserve the branching. Richly scented golden-yellow spidery flowers cover the leafless branches through midwinter into early spring.

planting.note: Plant grafted specimens in early spring or fall in moist, well-drained ground.

directions: These grafted hybrids should be planted with the graft union at or just below the soil line and given room to reach full width. Keep the roots cool with mulch, water through dry summers, and rub off any suckers rising from below the graft. Fragrant strap-shaped flowers in yellow, orange, or red open on bare branches from midwinter to early spring, followed by strong autumn leaf color.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in early spring or fall as a specimen shrub.

directions: Give this large late-blooming cultivar an open, sunny spot and plant it with the graft union near soil level. Mulch the roots, water in dry weather, and remove suckers below the graft to keep the named variety true. Clear yellow, sweetly scented flowers open in late winter and the foliage turns golden in fall.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant this red-flowered selection where winter light will backlight the blooms and keep the graft union close to the surface. Mulch to conserve moisture and prune only lightly after flowering to open the center. Coppery-red ribbon flowers appear in late winter and the leaves color richly orange-red in autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted plants in spring or fall in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: This wide-spreading cultivar wants space, so allow for a broad frame and plant at nursery depth with the graft near the soil line. Water during establishment and mulch the root zone; prune only to shape after bloom. Warm copper-orange flowers glow along the bare stems in midwinter and the fall foliage turns fiery orange and red.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in a sheltered, sunny position.

directions: Set this heavily flowering selection in moist, well-drained soil and keep the graft union near the surface. Mulch and water through the first summers, and prune sparingly after the flowers fade. Dense, sulfur-pale yellow blooms smother the branches in late winter with a strong sweet fragrance.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in spring or fall in well-drained, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant this vigorous yellow cultivar with the graft at soil level and mulch to keep the roots cool and moist. Water it well until established and thin crossing branches only after bloom. Abundant pale yellow flowers open late in the witch hazel season and last a long time in cool weather.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained ground in sun or light shade.

directions: Position this upright red-flowered form where its winter color reads against evergreens and plant it at nursery depth. Keep it mulched and evenly watered while young, removing suckers below the graft. Coppery red-brown flowers open in late winter and the foliage turns orange-red before falling.

planting.note: Plant grafted specimens in spring or fall in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Give this large cultivar room to develop a broad crown and plant with the graft union near the soil line. Mulch the roots, water through dry summers, and prune only lightly after bloom. Large fragrant flowers blend yellow, orange, and rosy tints along the winter branches.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil.

directions: Plant this golden cultivar at nursery depth with the graft close to the surface and mulch generously. Water it during establishment and prune sparingly, just enough to keep an open habit. Rich golden-yellow, sweetly scented flowers cover the bare branches in late winter and the leaves turn yellow-orange in fall.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in spring or fall in a sunny, sheltered position.

directions: Set this fiery cultivar, also sold as Magic Fire, where winter sun will light the blooms and plant with the graft near soil level. Mulch, water while young, and remove any growth from below the graft. Coppery orange-red flowers with a warm glow open along the branches in late winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained ground.

directions: Plant this late, free-flowering yellow form at nursery depth with the graft union near the surface and mulch the roots. Water through dry spells and prune only to shape after the flowers finish. Bright yellow blooms open toward the end of the witch hazel season and hold well in cool weather.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Give this early cultivar a sunny, sheltered site and plant it with the graft near the soil line. Mulch and water while it settles in, and keep pruning to a minimum after flowering. Fragrant orange-yellow flowers open early in the season and the leaves color well in autumn.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: This upright late-flowering cultivar suits smaller gardens; plant it at nursery depth with the graft close to the surface. Mulch, water during dry weather, and prune lightly after bloom to keep the tidy shape. Clean pale yellow flowers open late in the season and last well into spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a sunny spot with moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this vigorous, free-flowering cultivar with the graft union near soil level and mulch the root area. Water it through establishment and prune only to open the framework after bloom. Large orange-yellow flowers flushed with copper cover the branches in late winter, and fall color is a strong orange.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in spring or fall in well-drained, humus-rich soil.

directions: Set this warm-toned cultivar in full sun and plant at nursery depth with the graft near the surface. Mulch the roots, water while young, and prune sparingly after flowering. Deep orange-red flowers open in late winter and the foliage turns orange and red in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil.

directions: Plant this red-flowered form where winter light will catch the blooms and keep the graft union close to soil level. Mulch and water during establishment, removing suckers from below the graft. Reddish flowers open in late winter and the leaves color richly before dropping in fall.

planting.note: Plant grafted plants in spring or fall in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Give this deep red cultivar a sunny position and plant it with the graft near the soil surface. Keep it mulched and watered while young and prune lightly after bloom to maintain shape. Dense red flowers cover the bare branches in late winter and hold their color well.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained ground in full sun.

directions: Plant this compact red-flowered selection at nursery depth with the graft union near the surface and mulch the roots. Water during dry weather and prune only to shape after the flowers fade. Bright red ribbon flowers open in late winter followed by orange-red autumn foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant this bright yellow cultivar with the graft close to soil level and mulch to conserve moisture. Water while establishing and prune sparingly after bloom. Clear lemon-yellow flowers, only lightly scented, brighten the bare winter branches.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Set this strong red-flowered form in full sun and plant with the graft union near the surface. Mulch, water during establishment, and rub off suckers below the graft. Large coppery-red flowers open along the branches in late winter and the leaves turn warm shades in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant this warm bicolor cultivar at nursery depth with the graft near soil level and mulch the roots. Water it through the first summers and prune only lightly after bloom. Orange-red flowers with yellow tips smolder along the winter branches and the foliage colors brightly in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in neutral to acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Give this spreading Chinese witch hazel selection a sheltered spot and plant at nursery depth in moist but well-drained soil. Mulch with leaf mold and prune only sparingly after flowering. Large golden-yellow flowers with a powerful sweet fragrance open on the bare branches in midwinter.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in spring or fall in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this fragrant Chinese witch hazel with the root collar at soil level in a spot protected from harsh wind. Mulch the roots and prune only to shape after bloom. Soft yellow, richly scented flowers cover the leafless branches in late winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist soil where its suckers have room.

directions: Grown as much for its foliage as its flowers, plant this selection at nursery depth in moist ground and mulch the roots. Remove unwanted suckers to control spread and give it full sun for the best leaf color. Small yellow flowers open in late winter, new leaves emerge plum-purple, and the autumn foliage blazes orange and red.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in consistently moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this Ozark witch hazel selection in moist soil at the depth it grew in the pot and mulch to keep the roots cool. Thin out excess suckers each year to hold its shape. Unusually pinkish-purple to reddish flowers open in late winter with the characteristic vernal-witch-hazel scent.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich soil; suits smaller gardens.

directions: This compact form of the native witch hazel fits small spaces; plant it at nursery depth in moist soil and mulch the roots. Water during dry spells and prune only lightly to shape. Fragrant yellow flowers open in autumn as the leaves turn golden and drop.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant this late-blooming native selection at the depth it grew in the nursery and spread mulch over the root zone. Water it through establishment and prune only after flowering if needed. Clear yellow flowers open late in fall, often lingering after the leaves have dropped for a longer show.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in spring or fall in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this upright, late-blooming variety at nursery depth in moist, well-drained soil and mulch the root zone. Water during dry weather while it establishes and prune only lightly to shape. Pale lemon-yellow flowers open near the end of the witch hazel season, extending winter bloom into early spring.

planting.note: Plant out in spring after frosts, giving room for its lax, spreading habit.

directions: Set the plant slightly deep and shelter it from cutting winds that can scorch the thin foliage. Cut frost-damaged stems back to live wood in spring to encourage fresh growth. Dainty scarlet-and-violet blooms with a graceful drooping poise carry on through summer and autumn.

planting.note: Plant after frosts in a spot with light afternoon shade.

directions: Bury the crown a few inches deep for winter insurance and water steadily during dry spells. Prune out winter-killed stems in spring once new buds swell. The very pale pink-white flowers are smaller than the species and appear all summer into fall.

planting.note: One of the toughest hardy fuchsias; plant out in spring for a fast informal hedge.

directions: Plant deeply and space closely if you want a flowering hedge, watering well the first season. Shear frosted stems to near ground level in spring where it dies back. Masses of small scarlet-and-purple lanterns cover the vigorous bush from early summer to the first frosts.

planting.note: A reliable hardy border fuchsia; plant out after frosts.

directions: Set the base a little below soil level and mulch for winter protection in colder gardens. Cut back to live wood each spring and feed as growth resumes. Large flowers with scarlet sepals and violet skirts appear freely from summer well into autumn.

planting.note: Grown for its lime-gold leaves and red stems; plant in spring.

directions: Plant slightly deep and give some sun to keep the yellow-green foliage bright without scorching. Trim winter-damaged shoots in spring to shape the compact bush. Small red-and-violet flowers stand out against the golden leaves from summer into fall.

planting.note: A tiny hardy fuchsia ideal for edging and rock gardens.

directions: Plant this dwarf near the front of a bed where its small stature will not be lost, keeping the crown just below soil level. Light spring pruning keeps it neat and bushy. Little carmine-and-mauve flowers smother the plant continuously through the growing season.

planting.note: A pale sport of Tom Thumb; plant out in spring for edging.

directions: Set out in a bright, sheltered spot with the crown just under the surface. Pinch young shoots to build a dense little mound and remove frosted growth in spring. Semi-double flowers with red-pink sepals and white skirts bloom prolifically all summer.

planting.note: A near-white hardy fuchsia; plant out after spring frosts.

directions: Plant deep enough to protect the crown and site in light shade to keep the flowers pure white. Cut back frosted stems in spring and feed to fuel new growth. Slim white flowers faintly tinged green hang in abundance from summer to autumn.

planting.note: A compact hardy fuchsia with bronze-tinted foliage.

directions: Plant the crown slightly below soil level in a spot with morning sun. Trim in spring to maintain the tidy, bushy shape. Semi-double rose-and-white flowers stand against the dark young leaves throughout summer and fall.

planting.note: A hardy double-flowered fuchsia for borders and pots.

directions: Plant deep for winter protection and keep well watered while flowering heavily. Cut frost-killed stems to the base in spring. Full double blooms in pink and rosy-mauve open freely from early summer to the first frost.

planting.note: A vigorous, upright hardy fuchsia for the back of a border.

directions: Set the plant slightly deep and allow space for its strong upright growth. Prune back hard in spring to keep it well furnished with flowers. Semi-double blooms with carmine sepals and bluish-violet skirts appear over a long summer-to-autumn season.

planting.note: An easy, free-flowering hardy double; plant out in spring.

directions: Plant with the crown a little below the surface and pinch early shoots for bushiness. Cut back winter-damaged growth in spring. Neat double flowers with red sepals and rich purple petals bloom very freely all season.

planting.note: A tall, elegant hardy fuchsia with pale pink flowers.

directions: Plant deep and give a sheltered position for the arching stems. Remove frosted wood in spring and feed as new shoots emerge. Slender soft-pink flowers hang delicately from summer into autumn.

planting.note: A hardy fuchsia prized for its blue-and-white double flowers.

directions: Plant slightly below the old soil line and shelter from strong wind. Cut back to live wood in spring and mulch in cold areas. Large double flowers with white sepals and violet-blue skirts appear through summer and fall.

planting.note: An old hardy red-and-white variety for borders.

directions: Set the crown a couple of inches deep and water freely in summer. Prune out frost-killed stems in spring to renew the bush. Semi-double flowers with scarlet sepals and white petals bloom generously from summer to autumn.

planting.note: A dainty hardy fuchsia suited to informal hedging.

directions: Plant deep and space for a low flowering hedge, watering well the first year. Shear back to live wood in spring. Small single flowers with pale rose sepals and mauve-violet skirts cover the plant through the season.

planting.note: A robust Victorian hardy fuchsia with big flowers.

directions: Plant the crown below soil level and give a rich, moisture-retentive soil. Cut back hard in spring and feed for strong regrowth. Large single scarlet-and-magenta flowers, often several per leaf axil, open all summer into autumn.

planting.note: A versatile lax hardy fuchsia good in beds or baskets.

directions: Plant slightly deep in the ground or train the lax stems over a basket edge. Pinch to shape and cut back frosted growth in spring. Semi-double flowers with pale pink sepals and rosy-purple skirts flower freely all summer.

planting.note: A strong upright hardy fuchsia often trained as a standard.

directions: Plant deep and stake if training upward as a standard or tall shrub. Prune in spring to build a good framework. Semi-double rosy-red flowers appear in great numbers from summer to autumn.

planting.note: An easy, self-red hardy fuchsia for beginners.

directions: Plant with the crown below the surface and pinch young growth for a bushy plant. Cut back frost-killed stems in spring. Bright red single flowers open early and continue in profusion until the frosts.

planting.note: A borderline-hardy double fuchsia for beds and containers.

directions: Plant deep in the border or grow in a large pot moved to shelter over winter. Prune in spring and feed regularly. Large double flowers with rose-pink sepals and lilac-lavender skirts bloom over a long season.

planting.note: A neat double fuchsia, borderline hardy in mild gardens.

directions: Plant slightly deep and pinch the tips to encourage branching. Cut back in spring and protect the crown over winter. Rounded double flowers with red sepals and deep violet-blue petals appear freely through summer and autumn.

planting.note: A vigorous hardy fuchsia with large double flowers.

directions: Plant deep in fertile soil and water generously while in full growth. Prune out winter-killed stems in spring. Full double flowers with pale rose tubes and pink skirts are carried on a strong, upright bush from summer to fall.

planting.note: A self-branching upright cultivar good for shaping.

directions: Plant out after frosts and pinch early to build a well-shaped bush or standard. Bring container plants under cover before hard frost. Bell-shaped single flowers in clear pink bloom abundantly all summer.

planting.note: A popular trailing double for baskets; plant after all frost.

directions: Plant in a basket or pot of free-draining compost and pinch tips for a full display. Feed weekly through summer and overwinter frost-free. Big double flowers with bright red sepals and ruffled white skirts trail from early summer to autumn.

planting.note: A classic trailing basket fuchsia; plant after frost.

directions: Plant in a hanging basket and let the strong stems cascade over the rim. Water consistently and shade from hot midday sun to prevent leaf marking. Rich red self-colored flowers hang in quantity all summer long.

planting.note: A free-flowering semi-trailing basket cultivar.

directions: Plant several to a basket and pinch out shoot tips for a dense, tumbling mass. Keep moist and feed regularly through the season. Small semi-double flowers with white sepals and purple skirts appear in great profusion.

planting.note: A white double, upright or basket; plant after frost.

directions: Plant in a pot of rich compost and shade lightly to keep the flowers clean white. Pinch to shape and overwinter frost-free. Large double blooms in creamy white flushed pink bloom right through summer.

planting.note: An old double show variety; plant out after frost.

directions: Grow in a container of open compost and pinch early for a shapely bush. Feed weekly and shelter from strong wind. Big double flowers with bright cerise sepals and full white skirts open through the summer.

planting.note: A compact bushy double for pots and bedding.

directions: Plant in a container or bed after frost and pinch to keep the plant neat. Feed regularly and move under cover before winter. Full double flowers with pink sepals and lavender-blue skirts bloom freely all summer.

planting.note: A vigorous, easy semi-double for pots and standards.

directions: Plant out after frost and pinch to train as a bush or standard. Feed and water freely during the growing season. Semi-double flowers with bright red sepals and white skirts are produced very freely all summer.

planting.note: A tall, vigorous single fuchsia good for standards.

directions: Plant after frost and stake if grown as a standard, pinching to build height. Feed and keep evenly moist. Long single flowers with red tubes, white sepals and red skirts bloom over a long season.

planting.note: A triphylla-type grown for long tubular flowers and dark leaves.

directions: Plant in a warm, sunny spot after frost, as triphyllas take more heat than most fuchsias. Feed regularly and overwinter frost-free. Clusters of long, slender orange-red flowers glow above bronze-green foliage all summer.

planting.note: A triphylla fuchsia with dark foliage and orange flowers.

directions: Give this triphylla a warm, sunny position and steady feeding through summer. Keep it frost-free over winter to grow on the following year. Terminal clusters of long orange-red tubes stand above purplish-bronze leaves for months.

planting.note: A salmon-orange triphylla for warm, sunny spots.

directions: Plant in a sunny, sheltered position and feed regularly for continuous flowering. Overwinter under glass away from frost. Dense clusters of long salmon-orange tubular flowers top the velvety green foliage through summer.

planting.note: A tall species fuchsia with long red flowers and large leaves.

directions: Grow in a large container or sheltered warm border and protect from all frost. Feed and water well to support the vigorous growth. Long drooping panicles of slender crimson trumpets hang from the branch tips, followed by edible dark berries.

planting.note: A prostrate New Zealand species for rock gardens and troughs.

directions: Plant this ground-hugging species in gritty, well-drained soil where its trailing stems can wander. Protect from hard frost and standing winter wet. Tiny upward-facing yellow-and-purple flowers without petals are followed by showy magenta-red berries.

planting.note: Grown mainly for its coppery variegated leaves.

directions: Plant in a basket or pot where the lax stems can trail and give good light to bring out the leaf color. Pinch for fullness and overwinter frost-free. The gold, red and bronze foliage is the main show, with scattered red-and-purple flowers later in the season.

planting.note: A golden-leaved trailing sport of Marinka for baskets.

directions: Plant in a basket and give bright light to keep the foliage golden without scorching. Water steadily and feed through summer. Red self-colored flowers trail against the yellow-variegated leaves all season.

planting.note: A large-flowered trailing double for big baskets.

directions: Plant in a generous basket of rich compost and shade from harsh sun to protect the pale blooms. Pinch early and feed weekly. Very large soft-pink double flowers hang heavily from the trailing stems through summer.

planting.note: A trailing double with white and blue-violet flowers.

directions: Grow in a basket or pot and pinch for a full, cascading shape. Keep moist and feed regularly through the growing season. Full double flowers with white sepals and violet-blue skirts trail freely all summer.

planting.note: A vigorous, free-flowering upright for pots and standards.

directions: Plant after frost and pinch to train a bush or standard, feeding well for strong growth. Move containers under cover before winter. Bell-shaped single flowers with cerise-red sepals and rich purple skirts bloom very freely and early.

planting.note: A hardy semi-double with dainty red-and-white flowers.

directions: Plant the crown below soil level and mulch in colder gardens. Cut back frosted stems in spring to renew the plant. Small semi-double flowers with red sepals and white petals veined pink are carried freely from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Move plants outdoors once nights stay reliably above 45 F after the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, barely covering it, and keep the mix warm and evenly moist. Pinch young stems to force a bushy, well-branched plant and feed lightly through the growing season. The pendant, lantern-shaped blooms open from early summer until frost, and nearly year-round in frost-free gardens.

planting.note: Set out or train against a warm wall once frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant against a sheltered, sunny wall or let it cascade from a raised bed or basket, setting the crown level with the surrounding soil. Tie the lax stems to supports and shorten them in spring to keep growth tidy. The nodding red-and-yellow flowers, like tiny paper lanterns, appear from late spring through autumn.

planting.note: Plant out or pot up once the soil has warmed and frosts have ended.

directions: Grow in rich, free-draining soil or a roomy container, planting at the same depth it grew in its pot. Water steadily in warm weather and pinch the shoot tips to build a fuller shape. Bell-shaped orange flowers laced with crimson veins hang from the branches through summer and fall.

planting.note: Give it a warm, sheltered spot out of cold winds after frosts finish.

directions: Sow seed in spring and grow on in a sheltered, sunny position with sharp drainage, as it dislikes cold wet ground. This short-lived shrub grows fast and may need light pruning after flowering to stay shapely. Saucer-shaped mauve-blue flowers cover the vine-leaved branches in early summer.

planting.note: Plant into hot, well-drained ground once the soil has warmed.

directions: Set plants in lean, fast-draining soil and full sun where this desert native thrives on little water once established. Avoid overwatering, which invites rot, and shear lightly to renew the soft gray felted foliage. Cupped apricot-orange flowers open through the warm months and draw bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Plant against a warm wall in a spot protected from harsh winds.

directions: Position this fast, upright hybrid in fertile, well-drained soil with wall shelter in colder gardens. Prune lightly after flowering to remove weak wood and keep the framework open. Broad, saucer-shaped violet flowers smother the branches in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Give a sheltered, sunny site with free-draining soil after frost.

directions: Grow in a warm, wind-protected spot with sharp drainage where this tall Chilean shrub can reach for the sun. Thin out crowded stems after bloom to maintain an airy shape. Dangling, cup-shaped lavender flowers with a darker eye appear in late spring.

planting.note: Plant out or pot up after the last frost in a warm, bright spot.

directions: Set in rich, well-drained soil or a container and give consistent moisture through the growing season. Pinch early growth to encourage branching and remove spent flowers to prolong bloom. Cupped orange flowers veined in red are carried above the lobed, maple-like leaves from spring into summer.

planting.note: Train against a warm wall or plant in a large pot once frost has passed.

directions: Plant this arching hybrid where its lax stems can be tied to a sunny wall or fence for support. Shorten wayward growth in spring and feed during the growing season for the longest display. Nodding apricot-yellow bells with dark red calyces hang from purple-tinged stems from late spring to autumn.

planting.note: Plant out or pot up when frost danger is past and soil is warm.

directions: Grow in fertile, well-drained soil or a roomy container in a bright, sheltered spot. Pinch the tips of young plants to build a fuller shape and deadhead to keep flowers coming. Deep wine-red, bell-shaped blooms hang against dark green leaves through summer and fall.

planting.note: Move outdoors once nights are warm and frost has ended.

directions: Plant in rich, moisture-retentive but well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. Feed regularly and pinch stems to keep the plant compact and floriferous. Clear lemon-yellow lanterns dangle from the branches from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Plant out or pot up after frost, giving it bright light for best variegation.

directions: Grow this upright, vigorous form in fertile soil or a large pot in a bright position that keeps the leaf edges brightly variegated. Prune in spring to shape and feed through summer. Salmon-orange bells veined in red hang among the cream-margined leaves for much of the year in mild climates.

planting.note: Set out after frost in bright light to preserve the mottled foliage.

directions: Plant in rich, free-draining soil or a container in strong light, which keeps the yellow leaf mottling vivid. Pinch young growth for bushiness and prune lightly in spring. Salmon-orange, red-veined bells hang above the striking gold-splashed foliage through the warm season.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; give bright shade in hot climates to prevent leaf scorch.

directions: Grow this compact, boldly variegated form in rich soil or a pot with steady moisture and shelter from scorching afternoon sun. The heavily white-margined leaves are the main draw, so keep the plant well fed and lightly pruned. Soft orange bells appear intermittently among the cream-and-green foliage.

planting.note: Train on a warm wall or trail from a basket after frost has passed.

directions: Plant against a sheltered wall or let it spill from a container, tying in the slender stems as needed. Keep it in bright light to hold the yellow leaf mottling and prune to shape in spring. Red-and-yellow lantern flowers dangle among the flecked leaves from late spring to autumn.

planting.note: Plant out or pot up after the last frost in a sunny spot.

directions: Grow this dwarf, free-flowering selection in rich, well-drained soil or a container where its compact habit suits small spaces. Pinch early to keep it tidy and deadhead to extend bloom. Large, upward-facing pale yellow bells cover the plant from spring through fall.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in full sun to light shade.

directions: Set in fertile, moisture-retentive soil or a pot and water steadily through the growing season. Pinch the tips for a bushy plant and prune in early spring to control size. Pure white, bell-shaped flowers hang against dark green leaves for much of the year in frost-free gardens.

planting.note: Move outdoors after frost into a bright, sheltered position.

directions: Grow this compact selection in rich soil or a container with even moisture and regular feeding. Pinch young growth to keep it dense and remove faded blooms to prolong flowering. Warm coral-orange bells netted with red veins appear over a long summer-to-fall season.

planting.note: Plant out or pot up after frost in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil or a large container in full sun to light shade. Feed through summer and prune in spring to shape and renew the plant. Rich orange-red bells veined in deeper red hang among the maple-like leaves from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Set out after frost into rich, well-drained ground or a pot.

directions: Grow in fertile soil with good drainage and steady moisture through the warm season. Pinch to encourage branching and cut back in spring to keep it vigorous. Large, deep coral-red bells hang from the arching branches over a long bloom season.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a bright, sheltered spot.

directions: Plant in rich, moisture-retentive but well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade. Feed regularly and pinch stems to build a bushier plant. Deep rose-red, bell-shaped flowers dangle among the green leaves from summer through fall.

planting.note: Plant out or pot up after frost in full sun for the boldest color.

directions: Grow in fertile, well-drained soil or a container with regular water through the warm months. Pinch the young stems for fullness and deadhead to keep the show going. Golden-orange bells dramatically netted with red veins appear from late spring until frost.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a warm, bright position.

directions: Plant in rich, free-draining soil or a pot and water steadily during active growth. Pinch to shape and remove spent flowers to prolong the display. Bold red-veined yellow bells with a lantern-like calyx hang from the branches through summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a sunny, sheltered spot.

directions: Grow in fertile soil or a container with good drainage and consistent moisture. Pinch early growth and cut back in spring to maintain a compact form. Very dark, blackish-red bells hang against deep green leaves over a long summer-to-fall season.

planting.note: Move outdoors after frost into a bright, sheltered position.

directions: Plant in rich, well-drained soil or a large pot in full sun to light shade. Feed during the growing season and pinch stems to encourage a fuller shape. Soft peach-orange bells hang gracefully from the arching branches for much of the year in mild climates.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in bright light to hold the leaf variegation.

directions: Grow this compact, gold-mottled selection in rich soil or a container in strong light to keep the foliage bright. Pinch to keep it bushy and prune lightly in spring. Orange bells veined in red hang among the yellow-flecked leaves through summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in bright light for the best foliage color.

directions: Grow in fertile, well-drained soil or a pot in strong light, which keeps the mottled leaves colorful. Pinch young stems for a compact plant and feed through the season. Yellow bells with red veining hang among the gold-marked foliage over a long bloom period.

planting.note: Move seedlings outdoors after the last frost once nights stay warm.

directions: Start seed indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost, pressing it into the surface and keeping it warm. This dwarf, seed-grown series branches on its own and flowers the first year without pinching. Large upward-facing red bells appear from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Set seedlings out after frost once the soil has warmed.

directions: Sow indoors on the surface of a warm, moist mix 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost. Grow the compact seedlings on and set them out after frost for container or bed color. Big, clear yellow bells nod above the foliage from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Move outdoors after the last frost into full sun.

directions: Start seed indoors 8 to 10 weeks ahead, barely covering it and keeping the mix warm. This tidy dwarf blooms the first year and needs no pinching to stay full. Soft rose-pink bells open from early summer through frost.

planting.note: Set seedlings out after frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow on the surface of a warm mix indoors 8 to 10 weeks before the last frost and keep evenly moist. Grow the compact plants on for containers or the front of a border. Creamy white to pale yellow bells appear from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Plant out or pot up after frost in a bright, sunny spot.

directions: Grow this very dwarf, self-branching selection in rich soil or a container with steady moisture. Its naturally compact habit needs little pinching to stay tidy and full. Cheerful yellow bells cover the mounded plant from spring through fall.

planting.note: Set out after frost into full sun or light shade.

directions: Plant this compact, tidy selection in fertile, well-drained soil or a pot and water regularly through the warm season. It branches on its own and stays low without much pruning. Deep red bells dangle from the mounded plant from spring into autumn.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a bright, warm spot.

directions: Grow in rich soil or a container with even moisture in full sun to light shade. This dwarf form keeps a neat mound with minimal pinching. Bright tangerine-orange bells nod above the foliage from spring through fall.

planting.note: Give a warm, sheltered site with sharp drainage after frosts end.

directions: Plant this tall, fast-growing form in fertile, free-draining soil in a wind-sheltered, sunny spot. It is short-lived, so take cuttings to keep it going, and prune lightly after flowering. Large, saucer-shaped white flowers cover the vine-leaved branches in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in a warm, sheltered position with free-draining soil after frost.

directions: Set this large-flowered selection in fertile, sharply drained soil with shelter from cold winds. Grow it fast and prune after bloom to keep the tall stems from becoming leggy. Big, saucer-shaped mauve flowers smother the branches in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant against a warm wall in a wind-protected spot after frost.

directions: Position this vigorous, upright hybrid in well-drained soil with wall shelter in colder gardens. Prune lightly after flowering to keep the framework open and remove any frost-damaged wood in spring. Deep violet, saucer-shaped flowers cover the shrub in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Give a sheltered, sunny site with sharp drainage after frost.

directions: Plant in fertile, free-draining soil with wall shelter where cold winds are a problem. Thin crowded stems after bloom and tidy the framework in spring. Rich violet, open-faced flowers cloak the fast-growing branches in late spring.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in bright light to keep the foliage marked.

directions: Grow this gold-mottled form in rich, well-drained soil or a container in strong light. Pinch young growth for fullness and feed through the season for the best leaf color. Orange bells veined in red hang among the yellow-speckled leaves through summer and fall.

planting.note: Move outdoors after frost into a warm, sunny position.

directions: Plant in fertile soil or a container with steady moisture and regular feeding through the warm season. Pinch to keep it compact and deadhead to prolong flowering. Warm orange bells with red veins are held above the foliage from summer into fall.

planting.note: Move rooted plants outdoors only after all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Root a semi-hardwood cutting in moist media or set a potted plant so the root ball sits level with the surrounding soil. Feed heavily and water often through the growing season, and pinch the tips to build a bushy frame. Pendulous white trumpets open in flushes from summer into autumn, most fragrant in the evening.

planting.note: Move outdoors after the last frost into a warm, sheltered position.

directions: Root a stem cutting easily in water or moist soil, then pot up and plant out with the base level with the soil. Water generously and feed often, as this species is a vigorous, thirsty grower. Sweetly scented white to pale-pink trumpets pour out from summer to frost, perfuming the garden after dusk.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; give it room, as it grows into a large specimen.

directions: Set a rooted cutting or nursery plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and stake the trunk while young. Water deeply and feed regularly to support the extra-long flowers this species is known for. Trumpets open white and age to soft peach, reaching well over a foot long and blooming from summer into fall.

planting.note: Warmth-loving; wait for settled warm nights before planting outdoors.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in rich, free-draining soil kept evenly moist through the season. Provide steady feeding and protection from cold, as this warm-climate species resents chill. Long, slender white to pink trumpets with recurved tips hang gracefully from summer to frost.

planting.note: This hybrid is grown from cuttings and set out after frost.

directions: Root a cutting from an existing plant, since this garden hybrid rarely comes true from seed. Grow in fertile soil with abundant water and regular feeding for lush growth. Very large, fragrant white trumpets open from summer to fall and scent the evening air.

planting.note: Propagate from cuttings and plant out once frost danger has passed.

directions: Root a cutting or plant a container specimen in rich, moist soil and stake the trunk as it lengthens. Water heavily and feed often, and prune after a bloom cycle to trigger the next flush. Enormous golden-apricot trumpets dangle in profusion from summer into autumn with a strong evening fragrance.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; set outdoors after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in fertile, well-drained soil kept consistently moist through summer. Feed generously and pinch young growth to encourage branching. Peachy-apricot trumpets with elongated recurved tips hang like ornaments from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: An old, reliable cultivar propagated by cuttings and planted out after frost.

directions: Root a cutting readily and grow on in rich soil with plenty of water and food. Stake the trunk and prune to shape once the plant reaches flowering size. Fragrant creamy-white trumpets that age to pale peach appear in heavy flushes from summer to fall.

planting.note: Cutting-grown; move outdoors when nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in fertile soil and keep it moist and well fed. Prune after each flowering flush to keep blooms coming and the plant compact. Soft yellow, fragrant trumpets hang in abundance from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Propagate from cuttings and plant out after frost.

directions: Set a rooted cutting in rich, moisture-retentive soil and provide steady water and fertilizer. Stake the growing trunk and pinch tips for a fuller shrub. Large trumpets open cream and blush to soft pink at the flaring edges, flowering from summer to frost with a sweet evening scent.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings for its variegated leaves; plant out after frost.

directions: Root a cutting and grow in rich, moist soil, giving light afternoon shade so the cream-and-green foliage does not scorch. Feed regularly and water freely for strong growth. Fragrant peachy trumpets set off the boldly variegated leaves from summer into fall.

planting.note: Cutting-grown; set out once the weather has fully warmed.

directions: Plant a rooted cutting in fertile, well-drained soil and keep it evenly moist. Feed often and prune spent growth to spur repeat flowering. Long, slender pale-pink trumpets hang in cascades from summer to frost.

planting.note: Propagate from cuttings and give it plenty of room after planting out.

directions: Grow a rooted cutting in rich, deep soil with generous water and regular feeding to fuel its large size. Stake the trunk early and prune to build structure. Very long trumpets open white and deepen to warm salmon-pink, blooming from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; plant outdoors after all frost has passed.

directions: Set a rooted cutting in fertile, moist soil and water and feed heavily through the season. Pinch and prune to keep the plant bushy and flowering. Trumpets glow in warm apricot-to-orange tones and appear in flushes from summer to frost.

planting.note: Sow after the soil warms and frost has passed.

directions: Sow seed about a quarter inch deep in warm soil in a sunny spot with room to spread. Water moderately once established; it tolerates lean, dry ground. Upright white to pale-lavender trumpets open in the evening from midsummer to frost, followed by spiny seed capsules.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors a few weeks earlier.

directions: Nick and soak the hard seed overnight, then sow a quarter inch deep in warm, well-drained soil. Give this sprawling, gray-green plant plenty of horizontal space and moderate water. Large fragrant white trumpets open at dusk from summer to frost, most striking in the evening garden.

planting.note: Start indoors several weeks before the last frost, then transplant out.

directions: Soak seed and sow a quarter inch deep in warm flats indoors, then transplant after frost into rich, sunny soil. Water and feed for continuous bloom and deadhead to prolong flowering. Large upward-facing trumpets in white, yellow, or purple open through summer to first frost.

planting.note: Sow after frost; established plants may return from the root in mild zones.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in warm, well-drained soil and give this wide, mounding plant ample room. Once established it is drought-tolerant and thrives on little water. Big fragrant white trumpets edged in lavender open in the evening from summer to frost.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in a warm, sunny bed and thin seedlings to give each room. Water moderately; the plant is undemanding once growing. White trumpets appear through summer, followed by capsules armed with notably long, stout spines.

planting.note: Warmth-loving; sow once the soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in warm, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun. This desert species needs little water and resents wet feet. White trumpets with a purple-tinged throat open in the evening through the warm season.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in warm ground in full sun and thin the seedlings. Water moderately; the lobed, oak-like leaves give the plant its name. Small pale violet trumpets open from summer to frost, followed by spiny capsules.

planting.note: Start indoors before the last frost for earlier bloom, then transplant out.

directions: Soak seed and sow a quarter inch deep in warm flats, transplanting after frost into rich, sunny soil. This compact strain suits containers and needs regular water and feeding. Double, ruffled purple trumpets face upward and bloom from summer to frost.

planting.note: Start indoors ahead of frost and set out when the soil is warm.

directions: Sow soaked seed a quarter inch deep indoors and transplant after frost into fertile, sunny soil. Keep this compact plant watered and fed for steady flowering, and it does well in a large pot. Double, layered soft-yellow trumpets open upward from summer to frost.

planting.note: Start seed indoors before frost, then transplant to a warm sunny bed.

directions: Soak and sow seed a quarter inch deep in warm flats, transplanting after frost into rich soil. Water and feed regularly and deadhead to keep blooms coming. Large pure-white upward-facing trumpets are fragrant in the evening and flower from summer to frost.

planting.note: Start indoors before frost and transplant out into warm soil.

directions: Soak the seed and sow a quarter inch deep indoors, moving plants out after frost into fertile, sunny ground. Provide steady water and feeding for lush foliage and continuous flowers. Double and triple-layered purple-and-white trumpets face upward from summer to frost.

planting.note: Start indoors ahead of frost, then transplant to a warm sunny spot.

directions: Soak and sow seed a quarter inch deep in warm flats, transplanting after frost into well-drained soil with room to spread. Give this broad plant moderate water and full sun. Large white trumpets flushed with lavender open at dusk and release a sweet scent from summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring once the danger of cold has passed.

directions: Set this upright grower in well-drained acidic ground and protect it from frost while young. Prune after flowering to shape and to encourage strong new wood that carries the next display. Masses of deep purple-blue flowers smother the canopy from autumn into winter.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Choose a bright open spot that shows off the large, silvery-hairy leaves held in bold pairs. Keep soil evenly moist but never waterlogged, and trim lightly after bloom to keep the habit compact. Upright candelabra spikes of violet flowers rise above the foliage through summer and autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a frost-free position.

directions: Give this Brazilian species deep fertile soil and steady moisture in its growing season. Stake young trunks until they firm up and thin crowded branches to open the crown. Flowers open near-white and age through pink to lavender, so a single plant carries several shades at once in late summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost where the bold foliage is sheltered from wind.

directions: Set in humus-rich, well-drained soil and shield the huge velvety leaves from drying wind. Water generously in heat and cut back after bloom to control size and force fresh shoots. Tall upright panicles of purple flowers tower over the foliage from late summer through autumn.
